skills.prompt: This topic covers Select two-dimensional shapes for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.6(A)). Students identify and classify two-dimensional shapes by their defining attributes: number of sides, vertices, side lengths, and angles. Vocabulary: two-dimensional, shape, side, vertex, corner, circle, triangle, square, rectangle, hexagon, trapezoid, rhombus. Common misconceptions include identifying by appearance only and not recognizing rotated shapes. Activities should include shape hunts, sorting by attributes, drawing, and comparing with Venn diagrams appropriate for ages 6-7. When creating content, emphasize hands-on exploration with physical shapes, measurement tools, and real-world objects that 6-7-year-olds encounter daily. Encourage precise mathematical language when describing shapes, measurements, and spatial relationships. Include opportunities for drawing, building, and measuring.

This topic covers Compare shapes using attributes for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.6(A)). This geometry and measurement skill develops spatial reasoning and measurement abilities. Key concepts include understanding shapes and properties, applying measurement tools, solving spatial problems, and connecting to real-world contexts. Students ages 6-7 benefit from hands-on exploration with shapes, tools, and visual models. Activities should include manipulation, drawing, real-world connections, and integrated problem solving appropriate for Grade 1. When creating content, emphasize hands-on exploration with physical shapes, measurement tools, and real-world objects that 6-7-year-olds encounter daily. Encourage precise mathematical language when describing shapes, measurements, and spatial relationships. Include opportunities for drawing, building, and measuring.

This topic covers Select two-dimensional shapes for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.6(A)). Students identify and classify two-dimensional shapes by their defining attributes: number of sides, vertices, side lengths, and angles. Vocabulary: two-dimensional, shape, side, vertex, corner, circle, triangle, square, rectangle, hexagon, trapezoid, rhombus. Common misconceptions include identifying by appearance only and not recognizing rotated shapes. Activities should include shape hunts, sorting by attributes, drawing, and comparing with Venn diagrams appropriate for ages 6-7. When creating content, emphasize hands-on exploration with physical shapes, measurement tools, and real-world objects that 6-7-year-olds encounter daily. Encourage precise mathematical language when describing shapes, measurements, and spatial relationships. Include opportunities for drawing, building, and measuring.

This topic covers Compare sides and corners for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.6(B)). Students count and compare sides and corners of 2D shapes. Sides are straight line segments; corners (vertices) are where sides meet. The number of sides equals the number of corners in polygons. Vocabulary: side, corner, vertex, polygon, straight, count, compare. Common misconceptions include double-counting and confusing with 3D attributes. Activities should include tracing sides, marking corners, and comparing shapes appropriate for ages 6-7. When creating content, emphasize hands-on exploration with physical shapes, measurement tools, and real-world objects that 6-7-year-olds encounter daily. Encourage precise mathematical language when describing shapes, measurements, and spatial relationships. Include opportunities for drawing, building, and measuring.

This topic covers Two-dimensional and three-dimensional shapes for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.6(B)). Students identify and classify two-dimensional shapes by their defining attributes: number of sides, vertices, side lengths, and angles. Vocabulary: two-dimensional, shape, side, vertex, corner, circle, triangle, square, rectangle, hexagon, trapezoid, rhombus. Common misconceptions include identifying by appearance only and not recognizing rotated shapes. Activities should include shape hunts, sorting by attributes, drawing, and comparing with Venn diagrams appropriate for ages 6-7. When creating content, emphasize hands-on exploration with physical shapes, measurement tools, and real-world objects that 6-7-year-olds encounter daily. Encourage precise mathematical language when describing shapes, measurements, and spatial relationships. Include opportunities for drawing, building, and measuring.

This topic covers Compare vertices, edges, and faces for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.6(E)). Students identify and count faces (flat surfaces), edges (where faces meet), and vertices (corner points) of 3D shapes. Key concepts include systematic counting, understanding that different shapes have different counts, and comparing shapes by these attributes. Vocabulary: face, edge, vertex/vertices, flat surface, corner, three-dimensional. Common misconceptions include miscounting hidden features and confusing edges with sides. Activities should include handling physical objects, marking attributes, and creating comparison charts appropriate for ages 6-7. When creating content, emphasize hands-on exploration with physical shapes, measurement tools, and real-world objects that 6-7-year-olds encounter daily.

This topic covers Identify faces of three-dimensional shapes for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.6(E)). Students identify three-dimensional shapes (cubes, rectangular prisms, spheres, cones, cylinders, pyramids) and describe their attributes. Key concepts include faces, edges, vertices, and relating 3D shapes to real objects and their 2D faces. Vocabulary: three-dimensional, solid, face, edge, vertex, cube, rectangular prism, sphere, cone, cylinder, pyramid. Common misconceptions include difficulty counting hidden attributes and confusing 2D/3D terminology. Activities should include examining real objects, building with blocks, and sorting by attributes appropriate for ages 6-7. When creating content, emphasize hands-on exploration with physical shapes, measurement tools, and real-world objects that 6-7-year-olds encounter daily.

This topic covers Identify shapes traced from solids for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.6(E)). Students identify 2D shapes from tracing 3D solid faces. Key concepts include face-to-shape connections (cube face = square) and understanding that different faces may produce different shapes. Vocabulary: trace, face, solid, flat shape, imprint. Common misconceptions include not understanding the 3D-to-2D relationship. Activities should include physically tracing objects, matching, and predicting shapes before tracing appropriate for ages 6-7. When creating content, emphasize hands-on exploration with physical shapes, measurement tools, and real-world objects that 6-7-year-olds encounter daily. Encourage precise mathematical language when describing shapes, measurements, and spatial relationships. Include opportunities for drawing, building, and measuring.

This topic covers Symmetry for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.6(B)). A shape has symmetry when it can be folded along a line so both halves match exactly. Key concepts include identifying lines of symmetry, determining if shapes are symmetrical, completing symmetrical drawings, and recognizing multiple lines of symmetry. Vocabulary: symmetry, line of symmetry, mirror image, fold, match, half, reflection. Common misconceptions include thinking all shapes are symmetrical and misidentifying lines of symmetry. Activities should include folding paper, using mirrors, completing half-drawings, and finding symmetry in nature appropriate for ages 6-7. When creating content, emphasize hands-on exploration with physical shapes, measurement tools, and real-world objects that 6-7-year-olds encounter daily.

This topic covers Select three-dimensional shapes for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.6(E)). Students identify three-dimensional shapes (cubes, rectangular prisms, spheres, cones, cylinders, pyramids) and describe their attributes. Key concepts include faces, edges, vertices, and relating 3D shapes to real objects and their 2D faces. Vocabulary: three-dimensional, solid, face, edge, vertex, cube, rectangular prism, sphere, cone, cylinder, pyramid. Common misconceptions include difficulty counting hidden attributes and confusing 2D/3D terminology. Activities should include examining real objects, building with blocks, and sorting by attributes appropriate for ages 6-7. When creating content, emphasize hands-on exploration with physical shapes, measurement tools, and real-world objects that 6-7-year-olds encounter daily.

This topic covers Rhombuses for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.6(D)). A rhombus is a quadrilateral with four equal sides. It is a special parallelogram. A square is a special rhombus with right angles. Vocabulary: rhombus, quadrilateral, parallelogram, equal sides, parallel, diagonal, angle. Common misconceptions include thinking rhombuses must be tilted and not recognizing squares as rhombuses. Activities should include sorting quadrilaterals, measuring sides, and drawing in various orientations appropriate for ages 6-7. When creating content, emphasize hands-on exploration with physical shapes, measurement tools, and real-world objects that 6-7-year-olds encounter daily. Encourage precise mathematical language when describing shapes, measurements, and spatial relationships. Include opportunities for drawing, building, and measuring.

This topic covers Equal parts - 2 and 4 equal parts for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.6(G)). Students partition shapes into equal parts and name the fractions. Key concepts include truly equal parts, naming (halves=2 parts, fourths=4, eighths=8), and understanding that more parts means smaller pieces. Vocabulary: equal parts, partition, halves, fourths, quarters, eighths, whole, fraction. Common misconceptions include accepting unequal parts. Activities should include folding paper, cutting shapes, shading parts, and sharing objects equally appropriate for ages 6-7. When creating content, emphasize hands-on exploration with physical shapes, measurement tools, and real-world objects that 6-7-year-olds encounter daily. Encourage precise mathematical language when describing shapes, measurements, and spatial relationships. Include opportunities for drawing, building, and measuring.

This topic covers Measure using an inch ruler for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.7(A)). Students measure length using standard rulers (inches or centimeters). Key concepts include aligning at zero, reading the endpoint, understanding units, and recording with appropriate units. Vocabulary: ruler, measure, length, inch, centimeter, unit, align, zero mark. Common misconceptions include not starting at zero and confusing inch/cm sides. Activities should include measuring classroom objects, estimating first, and drawing specified lengths appropriate for ages 6-7. When creating content, emphasize hands-on exploration with physical shapes, measurement tools, and real-world objects that 6-7-year-olds encounter daily. Encourage precise mathematical language when describing shapes, measurements, and spatial relationships. Include opportunities for drawing, building, and measuring.

This topic covers Measure length with objects for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.7(B)). Students measure using objects or non-standard/standard units. Key concepts include end-to-end placement without gaps or overlaps, counting units, and understanding that different units produce different measurements. Vocabulary: measure, length, unit, end to end, gap, overlap, longer, shorter. Common misconceptions include gaps/overlaps and inconsistent units. Activities should include measuring with various objects and discussing the need for standard units appropriate for ages 6-7. When creating content, emphasize hands-on exploration with physical shapes, measurement tools, and real-world objects that 6-7-year-olds encounter daily. Encourage precise mathematical language when describing shapes, measurements, and spatial relationships. Include opportunities for drawing, building, and measuring.

skills.skill: Customary units of length: word problems
skills.prompt: This topic covers Customary units of length: word problems for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.7(D)). Students select appropriate measurement units for different situations. Key concepts include understanding relative unit sizes, choosing sensible units (inches for small objects, feet/yards for larger), and understanding both customary and metric systems. Vocabulary: unit, inch, foot, yard, centimeter, meter, customary, metric, appropriate. Common misconceptions include confusing systems and not understanding relative sizes. Activities should include estimating, selecting tools, and discussing practical unit choices appropriate for ages 6-7. When creating content, emphasize hands-on exploration with physical shapes, measurement tools, and real-world objects that 6-7-year-olds encounter daily.

This topic covers Match digital clocks and times for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.7(E)). Students read analog and digital clocks and understand time conventions. Key concepts include hour/minute hands, reading digital displays, matching analog/digital, A.M./P.M., and telling time to appropriate precision. Vocabulary: analog, digital, hour hand, minute hand, o'clock, half past, A.M., P.M., noon, midnight. Common misconceptions include confusing hands and difficulty near hour changes. Activities should include clock manipulation, matching, and daily schedule work appropriate for ages 6-7. When creating content, emphasize hands-on exploration with physical shapes, measurement tools, and real-world objects that 6-7-year-olds encounter daily. Encourage precise mathematical language when describing shapes, measurements, and spatial relationships. Include opportunities for drawing, building, and measuring.
